coredata.dc:title: Core-shell structured polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 nanocomposite capped with graphene quantum dots as gatekeeper for irradiation-controlled release of methotrexate
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaolin
Shou, Dan
Chen, Chuanxiang
Mao, Huihui
Kong, Yong
Tao, Yongxin
coredata.dc:description: A core-shell structured nanocomposite of polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 (PPy/mSiO2) is rationally designed as the nanocarrier for methotrexate (MTX), a chemotherapeutic drug for cancer treatment. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are introduced to the outer surface of PPy/mSiO2, and it functions as a gatekeeper for the loaded MTX through the formation of H-bonds with the functionalized mSiO2. In the proposed nanocarrier for MTX, the mesopores in mSiO2 are beneficial for the accommodation of MTX, resulting in enhanced encapsulation capacity of the nanocarrier; on the other hand, PPy can effectively convert the near-infrared (NIR) light to heat. Under the irradiation of NIR light, the H-bonds between GQDs and mSiO2 are broken due to the gradually increased temperature, and therefore the GQDs cap is removed and consequently the encapsulated MTX is released from the nanocarrier. In this study, NIR irradiation-controlled drug delivery is achieved successfully owing to the synergistic effects of PPy, mSiO2 and GQDs, which opens a new window for the construction of smart drug delivery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Core-shell structure
Polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2
Graphene quantum dots
Irradiation-controlled release
Methotrexate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite
Encapsulation of MTX in PPy/mSiO2 with the aid of GQDs cap
NIR light-triggered MTX delivery from PPy/mSiO2/GQDs
FESEM and TEM characterizations of PPy/mSiO2
FT-IR spectra of PPy/mSiO2, GQDs and PPy/mSiO2/GQDs
NIR light-heat conversion ability of PPy/mSiO2
Blocking-off capability of GQDs for MTX release
NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX from PPy/mSiO2/GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the synthesis of PPy/mSiO2, the encapsulation of MTX with the aid of GQDs cap and the NIR light-triggered MTX delivery.
FESEM (A, B) and TEM (C, D) images of the core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2.
FT-IR spectra of PPy/mSiO2, GQDs and PPy/mSiO2/GQDs.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (A) and BJH pore size distribution curve (B) of PPy/mSiO2.
Time-temperature curves of PPy/mSiO2 (a) and mSiO2 alone (b) under the irradiation of 1.0W NIR light.
(A) Kenetic release curves of MTX from MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs and PPy/mSiO2. (B) Residual MTX in PPy/mSiO2/GQDs and PPy/mSiO2 versus time.
Kenetic release curves of MTX from MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs in 0.1M PBS of pH7.4 without and with NIR irradiation.
Schematic illustration showing the NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX from MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs.

head.ce:title: Core-shell structured polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 nanocomposite capped with graphene quantum dots as gatekeeper for irradiation-controlled release of methotrexate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicine, Zhejiang Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Hangzhou 310007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A core-shell structured nanocomposite of polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 (PPy/mSiO2) is rationally designed as the nanocarrier for methotrexate (MTX), a chemotherapeutic drug for cancer treatment. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are introduced to the outer surface of PPy/mSiO2, and it functions as a gatekeeper for the loaded MTX through the formation of H-bonds with the functionalized mSiO2. In the proposed nanocarrier for MTX, the mesopores in mSiO2 are beneficial for the accommodation of MTX, resulting in enhanced encapsulation capacity of the nanocarrier; on the other hand, PPy can effectively convert the near-infrared (NIR) light to heat. Under the irradiation of NIR light, the H-bonds between GQDs and mSiO2 are broken due to the gradually increased temperature, and therefore the GQDs cap is removed and consequently the encapsulated MTX is released from the nanocarrier. In this study, NIR irradiation-controlled drug delivery is achieved successfully owing to the synergistic effects of PPy, mSiO2 and GQDs, which opens a new window for the construction of smart drug delivery systems.

Core-shell structured polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 nanocomposite capped with graphene quantum dots as gatekeeper for irradiation-controlled release of methotrexate
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Methotrexate (MTX), a folic acid antagonist, is one of the most effective chemotherapeutic drugs for the treatment of a variety of tumors, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis and hematologic malignancies [1]. However, the cytotoxic effect of MTX is not selective to the cancer cells [2], it also brings side-effects, such as leukopenia, hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity and bone marrow suppression, to healthy cells [3,4], severely limiting the efficacy of treatment. Thus, stimuli-responsive controlled release of MTX is of great importance in terms of reducing the potential risk to human health. Accordingly, increasing attention has been paid to building a controlled way for MTX delivery after its oral administration [5,6].

In the past decades, novel hybrid materials and inorganic materials with new and attractive properties have drawn increasing attention. For example, cross-shaped Au nanostructures in oleylamine [7] and upconversion nanoparticles made from NaYF4:Yb, Tm [8] possess light-thermal conversion ability and can be used for near-infrared photodynamic therapy. Incorporation of nanomaterials into optogenetics can make optogenetics a more powerful neuroscientific toolkit with deeper light penetration [9]. Recent discovery on gene delivery utilizing multi-functional fluorescent carbon dots [10] and inorganic nanoparticles such as Au nanoparticles, iron oxide nanoparticles, and quantum dots [11] as nanocarriers has also been reported. Magnetic anisotropic particles [12] and nanoparticles of different shapes and sizes [13] have been developed for drug delivery. The composites of hydrogels and nanoparticles have been successfully applied in catalysts, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, biosensors and drug delivery [14]. The above examples indicate that nanomaterials with low cytotoxicity can be promising candidates for biomedical and clinical application in the future. Although nanomaterials have been widely used as drug carriers, the development of novel nanocomposites for controlled release systems still remains a challenging and urgent task for scientists [15–17]. Among the nano-materials used as drug nanocarrier, mesoporous SiO2 (mSiO2) is considered to be one of the most promising candidates for drug controlled delivery owing to its large surface area, tunable size and porosity, and suitability for modification and functionalization [18,19]. Of particular interest, the methodologies to cap the pores in mSiO2 using pore capping materials as gatekeepers have also been proposed to enable the control of drug release with specific stimuli [20–22]. For example, Lin's group reported the modification of mSiO2 with chemically removable CdS nanoparticles [23] and gluconic acid-modified insulin (G-Ins) proteins [24], respectively, and then the controlled delivery of the encapsulated drugs was successfully achieved with the stimuli from dithiothreitol and glucose.

Polypyrrole (PPy), an inherently conducting polymer, has received great attention in biomedical applications due to its high conductivity, outstanding stability and biocompatibility, and low long-term cytotoxicity [25,26]. More importantly, PPy nano-materials exhibit strong absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region with a high photothermal efficiency at 808nm [27], and thus PPy nanoparticles are considered as a promising photothermal agent for NIR light-triggered drug delivery. Recently, high-performance in vitro and in vivo NIR photothermal therapy of cancer using PPy nanoparticles has been reported [28,29].

In this study, a core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite is facilely synthesized, and then graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are used as a cap for effective encapsulation of MTX in the PPy/mSiO2. The novel drug nanocarrier, PPy/mSiO2/GQDs, was applied for NIR irradiation-controlled release of MTX. In the as-prepared nanocarrier of MTX, the nano-PPy core functions as a photothermal agent for the conversion of NIR irradiation to heat; meanwhile, the mesopores in the mSiO2 shell provide MTX molecules with a better accommodation. GQDs acting as a gatekeeper are introduced to the outer surface of the drug loaded PPy/mSiO2 through the formation of H-bonds. Under the irradiation of NIR light, the temperature of the nanocarrier is gradually increased due to the excellent light-heat conversion ability of PPy. As a result, the H-bonds between GQDs and mSiO2 are broken, i.e., the GQDs cap is removed from the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs, and thus NIR irradiation-controlled release of MTX from the nanocarrier is successfully achieved.

Ferroferric oxide (Fe3O4), pyrrole, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and methotrexate (MTX) hydrate were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ferric chloride (FeCl3) and sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (SCRC, China). Pyrrole was distilled for purification prior to use and others reagents were used as received. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2MΩ (Milli-Q, Millipore).

The morphologies of the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite were characterized with a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss, Germany), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet FTIR-8400S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were recorded at 77.3K on an ASAP 2010 specific surface area and pore size analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The NIR light was emitted from a FU808AD1000-F34 laser device (Shenzhen Zhonglai Technology Co., Ltd., China). A model UV160A UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for the determination of MTX concentration according to the predetermined standard curve.

The PPy-coated Fe3O4 nanocomposite was prepared according to the method previously reported [30,31]. In a typical procedure, 0.5g of Fe3O4 and 5.4g of FeCl3 were added to 150mL of ultrapure water, and then the mixture was mechanically stirred for 3h at room temperature for the accumulation of Fe3+ on the surface of Fe3O4 via the common ion effect [32]. Next, 20mL of SDBS aqueous solution (5.85wt%) and 0.3mL of pyrrole were rapidly added into the mixture, and polymerization of pyrrole occurs at the surface of Fe3O4 by using Fe3+ as the oxidant. After being continuously stirred for 12h, the products were magnetically separated, washed thoroughly with ultrapure water/ethanol successively and repeatedly, and dried at 60°C to form the PPy-coated Fe3O4 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The mSiO2 shell of the nanocomposite was prepared by the surfactant-templating approach previously reported [33,34]. Typically, 0.3g of the PPy-coated Fe3O4 was dispersed in a mixed solution containing CTAB (0.9g, the templates), ammonia aqueous solution (1.2mL, 28wt%), ultrapure water (80mL) and ethanol (60mL). After mechanical agitation for 30min, 0.9mL of TEOS and 0.3mL of APTES were added dropwise into the above dispersion with continuous stirring. After the reaction for 6h, the products were magnetically collected and washed repeatedly with ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively. Next, the purified products were redispersed in the mixture containing 90mL methanol and 5mL hydrochloric acid and refluxed at 75°C for 12h to remove the CTAB templates and Fe3O4. Finally, the as-synthesized core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite was thoroughly washed with ethanol and ultrapure water for several times and dried at 60°C. Here, it is noted that APTES is a commonly used aminating agent for SiO2, and thus the resultant mSiO2 in the nanocomposite is highly aminated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of MTX in PPy/mSiO2 with the aid of GQDs cap
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1g of the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite was dispersed in 40mL of MTX aqueous solution (0.05mgmL−1) for the encapsulation of MTX into the mesopores of mSiO2. After being magnetically stirred for 12h, the drug loaded PPy/mSiO2 was centrifuged and separated, and the encapsulated MTX was accurately quantified by UV–vis spectroscopy at the maximum absorption of MTX (302nm) [35]. Noted that the absorption spectra of MTX in the filtrate remained unchanged after 12h owing to the saturation of MTX in the PPy/mSiO2 carrier, and thus the encapsulation time of MTX was chosen as 12h.

GQDs were synthesized based on a bottom-up method reported in previous studies [36,37], and the MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2 was transferred into the GQDs aqueous solution (40mgL−1). With the aid of GQDs cap, the mesopores in the mSiO2 were blocked off, i.e., GQDs acted as a gatekeeper for the encapsulation of MTX in the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite. The encapsulation capacity of MTX in the GQDs capped PPy/mSiO2 (PPy/mSiO2/GQDs) could be obtained based on the following equation: Encapsulation capacity (%)=100m enc/m add, where m enc is the amount of MTX encapsulated, and m add is the amount of MTX added [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR light-triggered MTX delivery from PPy/mSiO2/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 20mg of the MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs was weighted precisely, and then it was filled into a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off, 3500) and subsequently immersed in 30mL of 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH7.4. The temperature was set at the physiological temperature (37°C). The drug loaded nanocarrier was exposed to the irradiation emitted from a NIR laser device (power, 1.0W; wavelength, 808nm) with continuous stirring for the conversion from light to heat. To quantify the released amount of MTX, 5mL of the PBS was taken out every 1h for the UV absorbance analysis and an equal volume (5mL) of fresh PBS was added to replenish it. The whole process showing the synthesis of PPy/mSiO2, the encapsulation of MTX with the aid of GQDs cap and the NIR light-triggered MTX delivery are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The cumulative release of MTX (%) was calculated by dividing the total amount of MTX released at time t by the encapsulated amount of MTX in the nanocarrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FESEM and TEM characterizations of PPy/mSiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The FESEM images of the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 2A and B. As can be seen, the PPy/mSiO2 exhibits a regular nano-spherical shape with a uniform size of ~400nm, and this spherical morphology is expected since the mSiO2 in this nanocomposite is synthesized via the surfactant-templating approach. The core-shell structure of the as-prepared PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite can be clearly observed from the TEM images of the PPy/mSiO2 (Fig. 2C and D). The light-colored PPy nanowires are completely shielded by the outer mSiO2 shell, indicating the successful formation of the core-shell structured PPy/mSiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectra of PPy/mSiO2, GQDs and PPy/mSiO2/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra of PPy/mSiO2, GQDs and PPy/mSiO2/GQDs. For PPy/mSiO2, the broad peak centered at 3441cm−1 is assigned to the overlap of OH stretching and NH stretching [38]. Obviously, the existence of NH in the nanocomposite could be explained by the amination of SiO2 with APTES, an aminating agent. The peaks at 2918 and 1635cm−1 are due to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of –CH2 [39] and the CC stretching vibrations of the pyrrole ring, respectively, and the sharp peak located at 1463cm−1 corresponds to the typical pyrrole ring vibration [40,41]. The two peaks at 1099 and 788cm−1 could be ascribed to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretch of SiOSi, respectively [42], and another small peak at 466cm−1 is attributed to the bending mode of OSiO [43]. These characteristic peaks of pyrrole ring and SiO2 imply the successful integration of PPy and mSiO2. For GQDs, the two peaks at 1764 and 1720cm−1 are due to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of CO from the carboxyl [37,44]. After the GQDs are introduced to the PPy/mSiO2 as a cap, the peak at 1720cm−1 is red-shifted to 1716cm−1, which might be ascribed to the formation of H-bonds between the carboxyl of GQDs and the hydroxyl or amino groups of mSiO2 [45]. Meanwhile, the peak at 3441cm−1 on the spectrum of PPy/mSiO2 is also red-shifted to 3427cm−1 after the GQDs are modified to the nanocomposite, which is identified as a result of H-bonds between the hydroxyl or amino groups of mSiO2 and some oxygen-containing groups of GQDs [39]. Here, noted that for the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs, a new peak at 1288cm−1 owing to the asymmetric and symmetric bending vibrations of –CH3 [46] is observed, which is the characteristic absorption peak of MTX, and the appearance of such a peak indicates that MTX is successfully encapsulated in the nanocomposite with the aid of GQDs cap.

N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms are recorded for further characterization of the as-prepared PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite. Fig. 4 shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distribution curve. According to the IUPAC classification, the isotherms exhibit a typical IV-type with a hysteresis loop (Fig. 4A), indicating a uniformly sized mesoporous structure accompanied by the occurrence of capillary condensation [47,48], and the BET surface area and the BJH pore volume of the PPy/mSiO2 are measured to be 375.27m2 g−1 and 0.05cm3 g−1, respectively. The pore size distribution curve also confirms that the average pore diameter of the PPy/mSiO2 is 2.92nm (Fig. 4B), further suggesting the mesoporous structure of the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite. Considering the smaller longitudinal molecular length of MTX (21.2Å [49,50]), the encapsulation of MTX into the mesopores (2.92nm) of the PPy/mSiO2 is reasonable, and the encapsulation capacity of MTX in the PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite is calculated to be 85.6% according to the equation mentioned in Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR light-heat conversion ability of PPy/mSiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Since nano-PPy in the nanocomposite can effectively convert the NIR light to heat [28,29], it is expected that the ambient temperature of the PPy/mSiO2 will be elevated under the irradiation of NIR. Fig. 5 shows the relationship between the ambient temperature of PPy/mSiO2 and the irradiation time (curve a). As can be seen, the temperature is elevated rapidly from 18 to 32°C in the first 10min, and then it is gradually increased to 38°C when the NIR irradiation is carried out for 40min. The temperature remains almost unchanged when the irradiation time is prolonged further, which is most likely due to the fact that heat is dissipated from the PPy/mSiO2 into the surrounding medium [51]. For a comparison, the time-temperature curve of mSiO2 alone is also recorded under the same conditions (curve b), and it shows that the ambient temperature of mSiO2 rises only 3°C within 40min of irradiation (from 18 to 21°C). This comparison clearly demonstrates that the light-to-heat conversion ability of the PPy/mSiO2 is greatly superior to that of mSiO2 alone, and therefore the photothermal efficiency and efficacy in NIR irradiation-controlled MTX delivery from the PPy/mSiO2 carrier could be significantly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blocking-off capability of GQDs for MTX release
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, by forming H-bonds with the functionalized mSiO2, GQDs are introduced to the surface of the MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2 as a cap. To investigate the blocking-off capability of GQDs for MTX release, release of MTX is carried out from MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs and PPy/mSiO2, respectively, at 37°C. Fig. 6A shows the kinetic release curves of MTX from PPy/mSiO2/GQDs and PPy/mSiO2. After 12h, the cumulative release of MTX reaches as high as 74.63% in the absence of GQDs cap, which is higher than that released from the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs (53.83%). Accordingly, the residual MTX versus time is also plotted (Fig. 6B). As can be seen, the residual MTX in the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs nanocarrier still remains 46.17% after the release is carried out for 12h, whereas almost half of the amount of MTX in the PPy/mSiO2 (25.37%) is observed. All these results indicate that GQDs can be regarded as a qualified gatekeeper and may partly prevent the loaded MTX from being released from the drug nanocarrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX from PPy/mSiO2/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX from the nanocarrier is investigated. Fig. 7 shows the kinetic release of MTX from the MTX loaded PPy/mSiO2/GQDs without and with NIR irradiation. With NIR irradiation, a quick release of MTX is observed in the first 6h and then it gradually slows down, and the cumulative release of MTX can reach as high as 75.17% after 12h. However, the cumulative release is only 53.83% after the same time period without NIR irradiation. The greatly enhanced cumulative release of MTX from the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs could be attributed to the excellent light-heat conversion ability of nano-PPy and the existence of GQDs cap. On one hand, the blocking-off function of GQDs is achieved via the formation of H-bonds between GQDs and the functionalized mSiO2, and thus the release of MTX is temperature-sensitive since the H-bonds could be easily destroyed by increased temperature. On the other hand, nano-PPy in the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs can effectively convert NIR light to heat, leading to the NIR-irradiation controlled release of MTX from the nanocarrier. The whole process demonstrating the NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX from the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs is shown in Fig. 8 .

In summary, PPy/mSiO2 nanocomposite is synthesized via a surfactant-templating approach and used as the nanocarrier for MTX by encapsulation MTX in the mesopores of mSiO2 with the aid of GQDs cap, which acts as a gatekeeper and exhibits blocking-off capability for the release of MTX from the carrier. The as-prepared nanocomposite is characterized by FESEM, TEM, FT-IR and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, respectively. Due to the excellent NIR light-heat conversion ability of nano-PPy in the PPy/mSiO2/GQDs, the drug nanocarrier is successfully applied for NIR irradiation-controlled delivery of MTX, and the detailed mechanism on the controlled delivery of MTX is also discussed. These findings of this work are beneficial for the construction of promising nanocarrier for NIR light-triggered drug delivery, opening a new window for medical diagnostics and effective therapy.

The authors are grateful to the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21401014), Science and Technology Department of Zhejiang Province (2017C33165), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171194, BK20140255), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (SH2016015) and the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education Ministry.
